titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Thomas Fowke Papers
titlestmt.author: by Sandra Markham

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday July 3, 2024 at 01:00

did.abstract: The papers of Lieutenant-General Thomas Fowke of Park Hill, near Sheffield in Yorkshire, cover the decade 1746 to 1755, with half of the material concerning his term as Governor of Gibraltar (1753-1755). The collection contains approximately 500 letters, documents, and official and personal papers including copies of many of Fowle's outgoing letters. The correspondence is with business colleagues in England and Ireland primarily involved in real estate transactions, with military officers and those who wanted to enter service, and with officials of the British government and local governments near Gibraltar, particularly in Spain and Morocco. Also present is a small manuscript map, likely drawn as an exercise by a trainee military engineer.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two series: I. Personal Papers, 1746-1755. II. Diplomatic and Military Papers, 1750-1755.

bioghist.head: Thomas Fowke (1690-1765)
bioghist.p: Thomas Fowke was born in 1690, the son of Thomas Fowke (1645-1708) and his second wife Mary (1650-1705) of Gunstone, South Staffordshire, England. He was married twice, first in 1721 to Elizabeth Ingoldsby (1705-1735), daughter of William and Theophila Lucy Ingoldsby, and then in 1741 to Dorothea Randall (1704-1784), daughter of Edward Randall of Salisbury, Wiltshire. Fowke had three children, Theophila Lucy (1724-1734), Sir Thomas Fowke (1744-1786), and Theophila (1745-1756).

Fowke's military career spanned 1702 through his appointment as governor of Gibraltar in 1752. Following the British Navy's defeat at Minorca in June 1756, Fowke was courtmartialed, charged with refusing to provide Admiral John Byng with reinforcement from the garrison at Gibraltar, and dismissed from the Army. Fowke was reinstated to the rank of general in 1761 and died in Bath, Somerset, on March 29, 1765; he was buried at Bath Abbey.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: No provenance information was provided by the auction house but a twentieth-century printed genealogical pamphlet with a biography of Sir Frederick Ferrers Conant Fowke (1879-1948), Bt., of Lowesby Hall, was laid in the first volume, and likely indicates a custodial chain. The pamphlet was added to the library's collection file for the papers.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Thomas Fowke Papers. The Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: The Thomas Fowke Papers were received at the library in two volumes which were described in the auction listing as "over 500 items, many papers folded to fit the volumes, some fraying at outer edges, dust-staining, tears along folds etc., contemporary sheep-backed boards, spines defective, worn," which did not adequately describe their condition. Many of the larger documents were missing up to an inch of their outer edges, many others were torn and broken along fold lines, and there was only partial chronological order within the two volumes, which were advertised as dating 1753 (first volume) and 1755 (second volume). When the material was removed from the decayed bindings, flattened, and foldered, it was discovered that the collection spans an entire decade from 1746 through 1755, and the contents were mixed, with both personal and government/military topics covered in each volume.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers of Lieutenant-General Thomas Fowke of Park Hill, near Sheffield in Yorkshire, and London, cover the decade 1746 to 1755, and half of the material concerns his term as Governor of Gibraltar (1753-1755). The collection contains approximately 500 letters, documents, and official and personal papers including copies of many of Fowle's outgoing letters.

While only focused on a few years of his life, primarily 1735 to 1755, the papers provide details about his operations, agents, and governmental colleagues, as well as the constant need for intelligence about the movement of ships, both naval and merchant, between England and Europe. Organized into two series, the first holds material related to Fowke's personal life, assets, and activities in England and Ireland. The second series covers his life in Gibraltar and is predominantly dated in 1755. While not giving a full picture of his reign as Governor, the papers provide evidence of some of the military, political and diplomatic concerns he faced while there.

The Thomas Fowke papers contain no family correspondence with the exception of two letters from his son Thomas, while a student in Geneva; also present in Series I is a related invoice from Theodore Desvories, who handled Thomas's school arrangements. Desvories is one of several Huguenot refugees who emigrated to Ireland and appear in the Thomas Fowke Papers.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Thomas Fowke Papers is the physical property of the Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the W. S. Lewis Librarian/Executive Director.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into two subseries: Correspondence, and Documents.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I contains letters and documents related to Fowke's personal activities in England and Ireland, in particular those involving his real estate in South Yorkshire where his estate, Park Hill, was located just outside Sheffield. The full story of his life there is not encompassed in the papers in this collection, but he did sell Park Hill in early 1754 as he was transitioning to his post in Gibraltar. His correspondence with local attorney William Whitaker reveals information about the management of the estate while Fowke was in London, where he also had a home, and Whitaker's folder holds a multipage inventory of the house's contents. Major William Singleton was involved in the Park Hill sale, and other real estate transactions occurred between Fowke and Thomas Threlkeld; the latter defrauded Fowke and other creditors (see Fowke's letter to Count Joseph Haslang). Other transactions are covered in the thirteen letters exchanged with Raymond Kane, an attorney in Dublin. London merchant Thomas Tierney was instrumental in arranging shipments of items including horses, fowl, wine, and a cabinet of shells, from Gibraltar to London; his folder contains a printed bill of lading along with other descriptive documents. Fowke's own household goods were also shipped and a folder of inventories and invoices for that material can be found in the Documents subseries. Other invoices and receipts in the subseries cover some of his purchases and the work done on his properties; notable are three extensive invoices for commissioned objects and services: from Elizabeth Godfrey, the leading female gold and silversmith in eighteenth-century London, from the London upholsterer Peter Des Champs, and from a scrivener, William Ward, who created and managed legal documents for Fowke in London. The folder for merchant Galfridus Mann holds a dozen letters, receipts, and other documents detailing their financial dealings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Regarding a gelding

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from attorney Jonathan Woodhouse.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: An account of funds owed him.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Cheveley was an attorney of Lincon's Inn.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letter to Cope from Fowke

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: After emigrating from France, Dejean became military leader in the Grenadier Guards and the Light Dragoons. Lieutenant-General Dejean died in Dublin in 1764.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The folder holds an accounting of cash expended by Lieutenant Desvories for "conducting Mr. Fowke to Geneva" for schooling between January and April 1755.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Fitzgerald wrote from Ballycar, County Clare.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: See also John Perfect's 1751 invoice in Box 1, folder 36.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Fowke was a cousin of Thomas Fowke.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes one letter from Fowke to James Rivers in Whitehall.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two letters in French from his son Thomas while at boarding school in Geneva, with invoices for expenses.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Gosling was a banker on Fleet Street.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Haslang was a count of the Holy Roman Empire and the Bavarian ambassador to England.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Kane was an attorney in Dublin and Mantua, Swords, Co. Dublin, and a land speculator.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Mann, the brother of Sir Horace Mann, was a merchant in London.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Mitchell was a banker in Dublin.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes Fowke's copy of his letter regarding one of the legatees of his wife's estate.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: William Moore was a cousin of Thomas Threlkeld; his daughter Elizabeth Moore was a legatee of Mrs. Fowke.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Perfect was a nurseryman of Pontefract, Yorkshire, and a Lieutenant in 6th Regiment of Foot Guards.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes an inventory for fruit and forest trees Fowke purchased for Park Hill; Charles Fitzgerald wrote a letter on the invoice in 1753.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Widow of Robert Dillon, 7th Earl of Roscommon (-1721) of Dublin.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Major Singleton wrote from London and the War Office; the letters concern both personal real estate matters (at Park Hill and Dublin) as well as military news. Also present is a letter to him from Edward Woodcock of Lincoln's Inn.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Thornhill was from Diddington, Cambridgeshire.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The folder holds letters from him to William Singleton and Whitaker regarding Fowke's Park Hill property.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Threlkeld was born in Morpeth, and inherited the family home in Tritlington, Northumberland, from his father. In 1748 he married Louisa Hunt of Popham and settled there.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Legal and financial documents covering real estate and investment arrangements with Fowke.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Tierney was a native of Limerick and wealthy merchant of London, who later became a prize agent in Gibraltar.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The writer was a sister of Fowke; his sisters were named Mary and Martha.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Warburton was a celebrated antiquary and Somerset Herald in the College of Arms, London.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: He notifed Fowke that he had completed his pedigree, which had "run out to an enormous size."

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Ward was a scrivener and a Principal of Staple Inn and Gray's Inn.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A detailed invoice for work performed for Fowke from 1750 to 1753.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Whitaker, an attorney in Worksop, Nottinghamshire, managed Park Hill, Fowke's house and estate overlooking Sheffield. He worked in concert with Robert Barlow, who oversaw the tradesmen maintaining the property.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The folder concerns the management and sale of Fowke's property; one of his letters enclose an accounting of all money paid out by Robert Barlow. Also present in the file is "An Inventory of the Goods of Major General Fowke left in his House at Parkhill" dated May 1753.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Woodcock was an attorney of Lincoln's Inn, London.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The Woodhouses were attorneys.

did.unittitle: Estate of James Hunt, of Popham, Hampshire

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Accounting of money proved as a credit to John Hunt's estate from October 1, 1740 to October 10, 1753.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inventories of glass and china, invoices for wharfage, carriage, etc., for household goods and furniture removed from Park Hill and shipped to London and Gibraltar.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: An invoice from John Wildsmith for drawing plans for house alterations. Wildsmith was a British sculptor and stonemason who was active in London from 1757 to 1769.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: For liquor, beer, Maderia, port, and other beverages.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: For glass in the gardens and garrets.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: For snuff (James Fribourg), silver (Elizabeth Godfrey), hats (M. H. Wagner), Windsor chairs (Thomas Pitt), and bookbinding (Thomas Williams), in London.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: For work on Fowke's London home.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: For medical care from Robert Bates, likely at Park Hill, during 1751 and 1752.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: For a sedan chair (Samuel Vaughan), carriage repairs (John Hill), and horse furniture (Baker), in London.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Invoice from Peter Des Champs, London.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The invoices and receipts here are primarily for land management, crop sales, rents, and repairs.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Multipage agreement concerning property in Ireland owned by Sir William Ingoldsby and others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Agreement with Thomas Threlkeld.

did.unittitle: Estate in the parish of Firbeck

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two documents describing land owned by Fowke between Rotherham and Doncaster in South Yorkshire, and a purchase agreement between Fowke and Edward Woodcock and John and George Thornhill.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is organized into three subseries: Diplomatic Correspondence, Military Correspondence, and Documents.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series holds more than half of the collection and reveals some of Fowke's administrative network of military and diplomatic contacts. The first subseries, Diplomatic Correspondence, features letters to and from government officials and offices in London and British consuls stationed around the Mediterranean; many of their folders contain more than a dozen items. The correspondents include Henry Fox at the War Office, Secretary of State Thomas Robinson, and naval commander George Forbes, Earl of Granard. Francis Aiskill in Malaga, Burrington Goldsworthy in Cádiz, and Sir Benjamin Keene in Madrid are among the eight consuls found in the series. Also in the files are letters from groups of local officials in Morocco and Cádiz filed together under those places.

The subseries Military Correspondence contains reports about military issues and the state of the regiments and recruitments; two men making reports are Major J. Molesworth and Captain Edward Windus in Newport, both of whom wrote from Ireland. Also present are various requests for military commissions and favors as well as letters of congratulations to Fowke on his promotion to Governor. The Documents subseries holds a variety of items that were not connected to correspondence in the collection, but their content is diplomatic. The war in America, though far from Gibraltar, was of much interest to Fowke and was mentioned in some of the correspondence, including letters he received from Joseph Popham and Robert Napier. Popham, writing from Cádiz, had just met the master of a ship from America who told him "people of that Country were in high Spirits that our Forces were marching to the Frontiers in three different parts." The war was also highlighted in some of the four narrative reports filed here. Fowke was active in the repair and renovation of the Governor's House in Gibraltar, a sixteenth-century Franciscan friary that had become the residence of the British governors in 1728. Documentation of that project and the names of the workers involved are shown in eight detailed invoices. Another item, the "Order of Session of the Peace," describes physical conditions in the town of Gibraltar and a set of decisions that were made to improve them.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Aiskill was the British consul in Malaga, Spain.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence with different men having that title.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Aspinwall was the British consul general in Algiers.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Banks was the British consul in Cartagena, Spain.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Filed here are letters to Fowke from Spanish officials in Cádiz.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Calcraft served as a clerk in the War Office from 1747 to 1756.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Castres was the British consul in Lisbon.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Clevland was Secretary to the Admiralty.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The commissioners were Zachary Philip Fonnereau, Thomas Walpole, and Merick Burrell, contractors for victualling the garrison of Gibraltar.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a letter from Dalrymple Diodati & Co., with an invoice.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Captain Desbrisay, born Samuel Théophile Desbrisay, was a Huguenot refugee who emigrated to Lisburn and settled in Dublin. He was a member of the Dublin Society for Improving Husbandry.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Edgcumbe, a Naval lieutenant and politician, wrote from Mahon.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: De Escoiquiz was governor of Oran.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Goldsworthy was the British consul at Leghorn and Cádiz.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Robinson was Secretary of State for the Southern Department and resigned in November 1755.

did.unittitle: Great Britain. Board of Inland Revenue

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fowke's letter to the Right Honorable Lord's Commissioners of His Majesty's Treasury.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Hay was the British consul in Lisbon.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Herbert was a government clerk, secretary, and assistant paymaster of Marines.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Keene was envoy-extraordinary and plenipotentiary in Madrid.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two letters written to the Gazette regarding a cruizer from Arzila that had been seized.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Maplesden was the commanding officer of the Fortune Sloop in the Mediterranean squadron.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Writing to report about the two Swedish and nine French warships that had arrived at Cádiz Bay.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Filed here are letters to Fowke and to James Read from various government officials in Arzila, Sale (Salle), and Tétouan (Tetuan); also present are letters from Isaac C. de Lara and Joseph Mattos.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Petticrew was consul general in Tétouan, Morocco.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Popham wrote from Cádiz.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Robert Main & Co., a Scottish company operating in Cádiz.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Rowley was rear admiral of the White, Royal Navy.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Possibly Joseph San Just, Coronel del Regimiento de Cordova.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Skinner wrote from Lisbon.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Tanevot d'Herbault was president of the Société fraternelle des anciens Représentants de la Commune de Paris.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A letter in Spanish, and a letter from Fowke to an unidentified recipient.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A cloth dealer in London, Dale hoped to provide uniforms for the regiments.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also contains a letter from the commanding officers of each corps: Dussaux, John Grey, Charles Colvill, and John Craufurd, expressing their concern over the lack of silver in the garrison.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Stanhope was an army officer and was styled Viscount Petersham from 1742 until his accession to the earldom in December 1756. From June 1745 until his death he was colonel of the 2nd Horse Grenadier Guards.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Napier was adjutant to the Duke of Cumberland.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Captain Richardson wrote from Namptwitch (Nantwich), Cheshire.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The recipient of the letter is unknown, but was not Thomas Fowke.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters with report of returns of the regiment.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Yorke was chief justice of the Irish Court of Common Pleas.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The subseries holds items that appear to be unrelated to correspondence in either series, but with content related to military or governance issues.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Copy of letter relaying advice from Secretary of State Thomas Robinson to consuls in Barcelona, Malaga, Cartagena, Algiers, Genoa, Cádiz, and Alicante.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fowke's accounts as Governor of Gibraltar.

did.unittitle: Invoices for repair to the Governor's House

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Eight detailed invoices listing the names of workers by profession, and the cost of materials including lumber, bricks, lime, sand, nails, bolts, screws, and stone used to repair the building in May and June 1755. Also present is an invoice from Daniel Campbell for his work between September 1754 and February 1755, and two letters related to the project.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A list of the Royal Spanish Navy ships including frigates, bomb ketches, fire ships, packet boats, chebecks (xebecs), and line of battle ships, with names and number of guns on each.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The map is hand-drawn and unsigned. Mary Pedley, in the Map Division at the University of Michigan's William L. Clements Library, suggested that it is a student exercise in cartography, writing that: "It has many elements of a standardized style of representation of natural and man-made features, important for military operations: fortifications, with grey for built structures, red for buildings in stone or masonry, shading for elevation of the glacis, tree-lined roads, river with bridges of two different types (red for stonework, dotted lines probably for wood), trees of different type, enclosed woodlands, marshland versus cultivated fields, shading for elevation." Peter Barber, former curator of maps at the British Library, added that "it is a typical, polished exercise map [and] could well have been drawn at the military academy at Woolwich established in 1741." He suggested its presence in Fowke's papers could be due to "an applicant for an engineering post in Gibraltar where there was a continuing and voracious demand for skilled military engineers."

did.unittitle: Account from Boston in New England

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: An account received from the ship William that had arrived in Cádiz with news of military activities in America.

did.unittitle: Extract of a letter from Cádiz

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A from London sent to Mrs. James Read regarding the British and French fleets.

did.unittitle: Extract of a letter from Cádiz

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A report on British, Dutch, and French naval activities in the English Channel.

did.unittitle: Extract of a letter from London

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A report on military activities in America.

did.unittitle: Order of Session of the Peace

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: True copy of an order issued at a meeting held in the Town of Gibraltar public courthouse to set up regulations for street cleaning by scavengers, adding drains to every house for sewage, and setting standards for weights and measures and the price of bread.

did.unittitle: Staff Officers at Gibraltar for the Year 1753

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Docketed as "Signals by beat of Drum for the Manual Exercise Evolutions & Firings"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A signed statement regarding a dozen half shiners (Gibraltar currency) lost by Jenkins.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Four contemporary sheep-backed boards with partial spines.
